Appr No. i3973000 (ESTA issuance fee) and Overseas travelers insurance

To attend the 4th RAPID Symposium “Regional Collaboration and Innovation against Antibiotic Resistance in Asia” at the Pasteur Institute in Nha Trang. The symposium provides the latest insights into AMR trends, strategies, and research across Asian countries from a One Health perspective. As a bioinformatician studying urban microbiomes and AMR through metagenomic analysis, participation will strengthen my expertise and ensure alignment with regional and global research directions.

The objective of this trip is to participate in the 2024 Korea-EU Poland Collaboration Promotion Program in collaboration with Dr. Paweł Łabaj's at Jagiellonian University. The focus is on analyzing primary data to explore climate change’s impact on antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and establishing preliminary research directions. Additionally, specialized training in metagenomic sequencing data analysis will enhance analytical skills.

[Settlement] A face to face meeting for the ANR-NRF grant project

Relevance of travel
to project and research

The travel is expected to accelerate integration of experimental and target-specific datasets, improve the performance and reliability of the AI discovery platform, and resolve administrative issues related to material and data exchange. It will also strengthen Korea?France collaboration, leading to more efficient joint research and timely identification of novel antibiotic candidates.

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online

It is necessary to have a face-to-face meeting for in-depth discussion regarding data sharing procedure and the 2nd year's research plan. In addition, a face-to-face meeting was planned in the granted proposal.

Antibacterial Resistance Lab - Team Member - Kyungbae Min (00467)
[Settlement] Attending the 4th RAPID symposium in Nha Trang, Vietnam

The 4th RAPID symposium focuses on the dispersion of antibiotic resistance in Asia. This symposium will present how antibiotic resistance genes are transferred within the environment and their effects on human health in the Asian region.
By participating in the symposium, you will have an opportunity to gain a deeper understanding of the current status of antibiotic resistance dispersion and learn how to decrease it.

Relevance of travel
to project and research

The RAPID project''s emphasis on surveillance is a cornerstone of the broader One Health approach, acknowledging the interconnection between human, animal, and environment. By closely monitoring the environment, we can more effectively predict and preempt the spread of antibiotic resistance before it poses a significant threat in clinical settings. In addition, this symposium offers valuable insight into meta-genome analysis within public transport systems in Korea.

By getting new data, the symposium helps shift the focus from a reactive to a proactive approach. By monitoring AMR in key environments, public health officials can anticipate and prevent outbreaks of resistant infections before they become major clinical threats. Furthermore, this is essential for developing comprehensive, regional strategies to combat resistance that can be tailored to the unique challenges of each country.

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online
this symposium is not available on online.

Antibacterial Resistance Lab - Team Member - Kyungbae Min (00467)
[Settlement] Attending the 4th RAPID symposium in Nha Trang, Vietnam

The 4th RAPID symposium focuses on the dispersion of antibiotic resistance in Asia. This symposium will present how antibiotic resistance genes are transferred within the environment and their effects on human health in the Asian region.
By participating in the symposium, you will have an opportunity to gain a deeper understanding of the current status of antibiotic resistance dispersion and learn how to decrease it.

Relevance of travel
to project and research

The RAPID project''s emphasis on surveillance is a cornerstone of the broader One Health approach, acknowledging the interconnection between human, animal, and environment. By closely monitoring the environment, we can more effectively predict and preempt the spread of antibiotic resistance before it poses a significant threat in clinical settings. In addition, this symposium offers valuable insight into meta-genome analysis within public transport systems in Korea.

By getting new data, the symposium helps shift the focus from a reactive to a proactive approach. By monitoring AMR in key environments, public health officials can anticipate and prevent outbreaks of resistant infections before they become major clinical threats. Furthermore, this is essential for developing comprehensive, regional strategies to combat resistance that can be tailored to the unique challenges of each country.

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online
this symposium is not available on online.

[Settlement] settlement for GloPID-R RAPID symposium in vietnam

Relevance of travel
to project and research

This travel contributes directly to the project’s goals by fostering global research collaboration on emerging infectious diseases amid climate change. It supports metagenomic-based analysis of pathogen evolution and AMR trends through strategic engagement with the EU and Pasteur Network. Outcomes will reinforce the RAPID framework for integrated genomic surveillance and future outbreak prediction, advancing One Health preparedness.

This travel will strengthen global research capacity by enabling in-person collaboration with EU and Pasteur Network experts. It supports real-time planning for the integration of shotgun metagenome and metatranscriptome sequencing, advancing surveillance systems. The outcomes will enhance predictive modeling of pathogen emergence and reinforce the RAPID initiative’s role in mitigating climate-related infectious threats.

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online

This travel is essential due to the complex, interdisciplinary nature of the planning required. In-person collaboration enables real-time feedback, technical alignment, and trust-building with EU and Pasteur Network partners. On-site workshops and hands-on discussions are critical for integrating metagenomics into surveillance, which cannot be fully achieved through virtual means.

Accommodation	2 night hotel ( amount 100USD)

In addition, tracking antimicrobial resistance in the Asian region will help us to understand the direction we should take in future research.

Antibacterial Resistance Lab - Team Member - Guinam Wee (00528)

To attend the 4th RAPID Symposium “Regional Collaboration and Innovation against Antibiotic Resistance in Asia” at the Pasteur Institute in Nha Trang. The symposium provides the latest insights into AMR trends, strategies, and research across Asian countries from a One Health perspective. As a bioinformatician studying urban microbiomes and AMR through metagenomic analysis, participation will strengthen my expertise and ensure alignment with regional and global research directions.

Relevance of travel
to project and research

This symposium directly relates to my research on microbial communities in urban environments and their association with antibiotic resistance. Sessions on surveillance, resistance gene transfer, and AMR in Asia intersect with my focus on city microbiomes and metagenomic analysis. Learning regional AMR trends and strategies will broaden the scope of my studies and enable integration of fungal and bacterial microbiome findings into international AMR.

Participation will provide up-to-date knowledge on AMR in Asia, strengthen international collaboration, and create opportunities for joint research. The insights gained will enhance my metagenomic analyses of urban microbiomes, increase the practical applicability of my projects, and contribute to expanding cooperative strategies against AMR.

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online
The conference is planned as face-to-face meeting without an online option.

Transport Fee	Around $400
Accommodation	Around $85 (2 nights)

Departure from Vietnam to Korea

To attend the 4th RAPID Symposium “Regional Collaboration and Innovation against Antibiotic Resistance in Asia” at the Pasteur Institute in Nha Trang. The symposium provides the latest insights into AMR trends, strategies, and research across Asian countries from a One Health perspective. As a bioinformatician studying urban microbiomes and AMR through metagenomic analysis, participation will strengthen my expertise and ensure alignment with regional and global research directions.

The objective of this trip is to participate in the 2024 Korea-EU Poland Collaboration Promotion Program in collaboration with Dr. Paweł Łabaj's at Jagiellonian University. The focus is on analyzing primary data to explore climate change’s impact on antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and establishing preliminary research directions. Additionally, specialized training in metagenomic sequencing data analysis will enhance analytical skills.

Antibacterial Resistance Lab - Team Member - Guinam Wee (00528)

The objective of this trip is to participate in the 2024 Korea-EU Poland Collaboration Promotion Program in collaboration with Dr. Paweł Łabaj''s at Jagiellonian University. The focus is on analyzing primary data to explore climate change’s impact on antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and establishing preliminary research directions. Additionally, specialized training in metagenomic sequencing data analysis will enhance analytical skills.

Relevance of travel
to project and research

The expected outcome is a comprehensive dataset revealing the link between climate change and AMR. Also, by strengthening international research networks, this collaboration will lay the groundwork for future joint projects addressing global health challenges associated with climate change and antimicrobial resistance (AMR).

Reason why this travel
cannot been replaced online

It is not possible to replace this travel online due to the necessity of in-person access to specialized laboratory facilities, analytical tools and data-sharing capabilities that are available at Jagiellonian University.

To attend the 4th RAPID Symposium “Regional Collaboration and Innovation against Antibiotic Resistance in Asia” at the Pasteur Institute in Nha Trang. The symposium provides the latest insights into AMR trends, strategies, and research across Asian countries from a One Health perspective. As a bioinformatician studying urban microbiomes and AMR through metagenomic analysis, participation will strengthen my expertise and ensure alignment with regional and global research directions.
